label: no
cot_prefix: Okay, so I need to figure out if an 8x8 chessboard with two diagonally opposite corners removed can be completely tiled with dominoes. Each domino covers two adjacent squares. Hmm, let me think. First, I remember that a regular 8x8 chessboard has 64 squares. If we remove two corners, there are 62 squares left. Since each domino covers two squares, 31 dominoes would be needed. So the number of squares is even, which is a good sign. But just because the numbers work out doesn't mean it's possible. There must be some other condition. Wait, domino tiling problems often have something to do with coloring. Like the classic chessboard coloring where each domino covers one black and one white square. Let me recall. On a standard chessboard, there are equal numbers of black and white squares, right? 32 each. If you remove two corners, which are diagonally opposite, those two squares would be of the same color, right? Because on a chessboard, diagonally opposite corners are the same color. For example, if you take the top-left corner, which is usually a black square, the diagonally opposite corner (bottom-right) is also black. So removing both would leave 30 black squares and 32 white squares, or vice versa? Wait, let me check that. Suppose the top-left corner is black. Then the squares alternate colors.
